title: From Ease To Difficulty
study: A Study In The Life Of Joseph
scriptureForWeek: GENESIS 50:14-26 After burying Jacob, Joseph returned to Egypt with his brothers and all who had accompanied him to his father’s burial. But now that their father was dead, Joseph’s brothers became fearful. “Now Joseph will show his anger and pay us back for all the wrong we did to him,” they said. So they sent this message to Joseph: “Before your father died, he instructed us to say to you: ‘Please forgive your brothers for the great wrong they did to you—for their sin in treating you so cruelly.’ So we, the servants of the God of your father, beg you to forgive our sin.” When Joseph received the message, he broke down and wept. Then his brothers came and threw themselves down before Joseph. “Look, we are your slaves!” they said. But Joseph replied, “Don’t be afraid of me. Am I God, that I can punish you? You intended to harm me, but God intended it all for good. He brought me to this position so I could save the lives of many people. No, don’t be afraid. I will continue to take care of you and your children.” So he reassured them by speaking kindly to them. So Joseph and his brothers and their families continued to live in Egypt. Joseph lived to the age of 110. He lived to see three generations of descendants of his son Ephraim, and he lived to see the birth of the children of Manasseh’s son Makir, whom he claimed as his own. “Soon I will die,” Joseph told his brothers, “but God will surely come to help you and lead you out of this land of Egypt. He will bring you back to the land he solemnly promised to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob.” Then Joseph made the sons of Israel swear an oath, and he said, “When God comes to help you and lead you back, you must take my bones with you.” So Joseph died at the age of 110. The Egyptians embalmed him, and his body was placed in a coffin in Egypt.
weekTheme: Boys think short term, men think long term God has a destiny for every man. We are not accidents here at random. God has good works prepared for us to walk in. But discovering that when you are young can be very difficult. With so much cynicism in our culture, so much anxiety about the future, and so much dread and disaster fed to us in the media, young men can struggle to find their place in the world. This week is the last week of this study, so we will be looking back at what we can learn from Joseph about the journey of becoming a man that leaves a legacy. The goal of this week is for your son to be reminded from the life of Joseph of the goodness of God to work out his plans for us and to leave a godly legacy, no matter what.

days.title: The Difficulty Of Vision
days.scripture: GENESIS 50:19-21 19 But Joseph replied, “Don’t be afraid of me. Am I God, that I can punish you? 20 You intended to harm me, but God intended it all for good. He brought me to this position so I could save the lives of many people. 21 No, don’t be afraid. I will continue to take care of you and your children.” So he reassured them by speaking kindly to them.
days.reflection: This is the last week in your study of the first shift in your journey of moving from a young guy into becoming a man. This shift was about moving from Ease to Difficulty. Boys love ease; men embrace difficulty. These last 10 weeks you have looked at the story of Joseph and the wild life he had. Joseph started in a place of ease, with a loving family and father who gave him a special coat. Joseph also carried a vision given to him by God. That vision included his brothers and father bowing down before him. That dream ended up trigging a series of catastrophic, heartbreaking, and destiny shaping moments. He ended up seeing his vision come to pass and was aware of God’s leadership and guidance toward that vision coming true. Joseph understood the power of vision. Having a vision is an incredible thing, and it’s rare. Young guys with vision have a real advantage over those trapped in the present. They can see beyond the moment. When everyone else has short term thinking, only wondering about the next class, the next game, the next day, guys with vision look further into the future. They think about what they will do for college, what they want to do for a living, what they may want in a wife, where they may want to live, what God may call them to do. They think long term, big picture, beyond the now. They have hope when others are cynical. You actually live at a really amazing time in history. There has never been a better time to live, with incredible possibility. God can take you anywhere and do anything with you. In other times in history, people were defined by massive limitation. They rarely traveled beyond their home town, and only did whatever job their family permitted. But now, you can travel around the world, get an education in almost any topic imaginable, and live where God leads. It’s a time to dream about what is possible and what God may have in store for you. They trust God’s call on their life. You are not an accident. You are here by God's special call and purpose. Ephesians 1 says that God chose you in Christ before the foundation of the world. Think about how little say you had in your life. You didn’t choose when you would be born, the family you would be born into, your ethnicity or gender, or your country. God chose those things for a reason, because he wanted you to be you, here and now. And God has good works prepared in advance for you to do. Just like Joseph was given a vision by God that guided his life, God has a vision for your life too. You should begin to seek him and ask him what it is. God speaks to teenagers all the time in the bible and uses them in amazing ways. He will begin to share it with you if you set your hearts on doing what he wants. You can be a teenager with vision like Joseph was too.
questions: Why do you think guys your age struggle to think about the future and long- term planning? Do you struggle with this?
Have you ever given any thought to the fact that you might have a destiny or vision for your life? If so, what thoughts have you had?
Ask the person you are doing this study with when they got a vision for their life or a sense of call. How did they discover it, and how did things change as a result of it?

thoughts.verse: For we are God’s masterpiece. He has created us anew in Christ Jesus, so we can do the good things he planned for us long ago.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for his calling and purpose for your life. Ask him to begin to give you a vision this week. Vision for your future and calling. Ask him to open your eyes to the good works he may have planned out for you right now.

days.title: The Difficulty Of Trust
days.scripture: GENESIS 50:19,21 19 But Joseph replied, “Don’t be afraid of me. Am I God, that I can punish you? 21 No, don’t be afraid. I will continue to take care of you and your children.” So he reassured them by speaking kindly to them.
days.reflection: Joseph went through some crazy times. You will too. You may have even been through some already. Crazy times reveal what we trust in. It’s easy to say that you trust God when things are going well. Good grades, cool friends, great hobbies, everything coming together. But good times don’t reveal trust; they reveal God's goodness. But when stuff goes crazy, that’s when you learn to live by faith and trust God. Trusting Him when you are struggling to understand core concepts at school, one of your friends is spreading rumors about you, or your family is stressing you out is another thing. As you look back on all you have learned from Joseph's life, one of the big takeaways was his trust in God. No matter what circumstances he was facing, he kept his eyes fixed on God and believed that he would work things out. Toward the end of his life, when he reflected on his rejection, betrayal, enslavement, and imprisonment, Joseph wasn’t bitter and resentful towards his brothers. He saw the hand of God in all of it. He writes: "You intended to harm me, but God intended it all for good. He brought me to this position so I could save the lives of many people." What a perspective! Putting our faith in God, even when things are hard, is a sign of real maturity. It shows courage and humility and reflects the heart of grown men. If Joseph were sitting down with you today, he wouldn’t want you to be impressed. He would want you to be inspired. He would want you to trust God with the situations you will face in your life that cause you stress and anxiety. Trusting God is what men learn to do. Freaking out is what boys do. In the New Testament, Paul the Apostle had an attitude like Joseph. He was imprisoned and persecuted for his faith, but he trusted that God would use it somehow, and God did. He wrote in Philippians 1:12-14: "And I want you to know, my dear brothers and sisters, that everything that has happened to me here has helped to spread the Good News. For everyone here, including the whole palace guard, knows that I am in chains because of Christ. And because of my imprisonment, most of the believers here have gained confidence and boldly speak God’s message without fear." Paul was in prison, be he trusted in God, and God worked things out to push the kingdom forward. If you can trust God in prison, you can trust him anywhere. God has shown us again and again through history that he can be trusted. He has shown us that we can trust his love, his provision, his plans, and his goodness, That’s why Psalm 9:10 says, “And those who know your name put their trust in you, for you, O Lord, have not forsaken those who seek you.” People can be inconsistent, change their minds, and turn their backs on us. But God never will, even in the hardest situations. You can trust God like this too.
questions: Do you think our world is becoming more or less trusting? Why or why not?
Who is someone you think you can trust? Why do you think you can trust him or her?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a story of how they learned to trust God when they were younger. What was the situation, and how did they learn to rely on God and not themselves?

thoughts.verse: You will keep in perfect peace all who trust in you, all whose thoughts are fixed on you! Trust in the LORD always, for the LORD GOD is the eternal Rock.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to still your heart and see if there is a situation or area of your life that you are struggling to trust God in. If there is, invite him into it. 1 Peter 5:7 says. “Give all your worries and cares to God, for he cares about you.”

days.title: The Difficulty Of Testing
days.scripture: GENESIS 50:14-18 14 After burying Jacob, Joseph returned to Egypt with his brothers and all who had accompanied him to his father’s burial. 15 But now that their father was dead, Joseph’s brothers became fearful. “Now Joseph will show his anger and pay us back for all the wrong we did to him,” they said. 16 So they sent this message to Joseph: “Before your father died, he instructed us to say to you: 17 ‘Please forgive your brothers for the great wrong they did to you—for their sin in treating you so cruelly.’ So we, the servants of the God of your father, beg you to forgive our sin.” When Joseph received the message, he broke down and wept. 18 Then his brothers came and threw themselves down before Joseph. “Look, we are your slaves!” they said.
days.reflection: As you go through life, you are going to have to pass tests. Lots of them. You are going to pass tests at school to get to the next grade. You are going to have to pass a driving test to get behind the wheel. And you are going to have to pass tests at college to move into your career. There is no way around it. Tests are a part of life. Now the tests mentioned above are the obvious kinds of tests we all think about. Natural tests. But there are other tests we will face in life. Spiritual tests. These are tests of our faith to see if God can trust us and use us. Passing or failing these tests determines the kind of legacy we will leave in each season of life. Joseph faced all kinds of tests in his story. He faced a trust test: would he believe in God when his circumstances were awful? He faced an integrity test: would he resist temptation when Potiphar’s wife wanted to have sex with him? He faced the delay test: would he remain faithful when things seemed to take forever? And now with his Father dead, he faced a power test: would he punish his brothers for what they did to him when no one else could stop him? Or would he be merciful and forgiving? Can you imagine what it felt like to be him at this moment? Having the power of life and death over his brothers. The ability to punish them or give them prosperity? Their whole future hung on the kind of man Joseph had become over the years. Joseph's reply shows he passed this test too. He tells his brothers that they have nothing to fear. He will protect and provide for them no matter what. Joseph has grown into a godly man, a man who can be trusted in every area of his life. That’s why God could give him so much power and use him as an influential leader to serve the whole nation. God often tests us in small private moments, before trusting us with bigger, public ones. Little things like telling the truth even when it hurts, doing something when you say you will do it, and getting your homework done on time may not seem big to you. But small obedience leads to huge trust. Passing small character tests like this will make you the kind of man who can handle more. More power, more freedom, more responsibility. Proverbs 17:3 says, “Fire tests the purity of silver and gold, but the LORD tests the heart.” The truth is, you are who you are when no one is looking. And if we can be trusted to honor God when no one is around, we can be trusted with a lot. God wants to help you pass the tests of life. He gives you his word so you can know what he wants, his Spirit to give you the power to obey, and his grace when you fall short. King David wrote in 1 Chronicles 29:17 “I know, my God, that you examine our hearts and rejoice when you find integrity there.” He rejoiced in Joseph's integrity, and he wants to rejoice in yours too.
questions: Do you normally get stressed out before tests, or feel confident? Which of your teachers gives you the hardest tests?
Have you ever been in a situation where you thought God may be testing you?
What do you think is the difference between testing and temptation? (James 1:13 has a clue)

thoughts.verse: For you know that when your faith is tested, your endurance has a chance to grow. So let it grow, for when your endurance is fully developed, you will be perfect and complete, needing nothing.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a heart to please and obey him in every area of your life. Ask him to strengthen you for any tests you may face today.

days.title: The Difficulty Of Ordinary Days
days.scripture: GENESIS 50:22-23 22 So Joseph and his brothers and their families continued to live in Egypt. Joseph lived to the age of 110. 23 He lived to see three generations of descendants of his son Ephraim, and he lived to see the birth of the children of Manasseh’s son Makir, whom he claimed as his own.
days.reflection: Lots of guys can fall into the trap of thinking that every second of life has to be amazing. We are trained by the culture around us to love that which is exciting! Incredible! Awesome! Most guys probably view boredom as suffering or sin. Yet it's impossible for life to be one giant high. There are a lot of ordinary moments involved in creating a legacy. Part of the challenge of reading about the lives of men in the Bible is that scripture compresses time. You see that here in this passage. About 70 years are covered in one short verse. “Joseph lived to be age 110.” And truth be told, we don’t know that much about what happened in these years. There were just thousands of days one after the other, being loyal to God and serving him well. This is important to remember as a young man. Life is long, longer than you think. And your long life cannot always be exciting. Yes, there will be some incredible highs and heartbreaking lows, but you just need to keep going and live one day at a time. Jesus spent 30 years in obscurity for 3 years of public ministry. 90 percent of his life was hidden. The book of Acts covers almost 40 years, with some big miracles and trips scattered throughout. The Bible itself took about 1500 years to be finished. That’s a long time to write a story down. Your legacy and life will not only be found in the big wow moments but in all the ordinary moments too. Just keep being faithful, walk with God each day, and learn to ride the waves as they come. You will look back before you know it with wonder and joy. Joseph moved from ease to difficulty and left a legacy as a skillful and godly man. I am confident that you will too.
questions: How do you tend to handle boredom?
Why do you think that good times go so fast and boring times so slow?
In what ways do you think the media distorts people’s expectations about what life is like?
Ask the person you are doing this study with how they have learned to find joy and meaning in the ordinary stuff of life.

thoughts.verse: Don’t let the excitement of youth cause you to forget your Creator. Honor him in your youth before you grow old and say, “Life is not pleasant anymore.”
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for the simple things in your life. The small areas of joy and faithfulness you see every day. Take a moment and list a few out and turn them into prayers of gratitude.

days.title: The Difficulty Of Legacy
days.scripture: GENESIS 50:24-26 24 “Soon I will die,” Joseph told his brothers, “but God will surely come to help you and lead you out of this land of Egypt. He will bring you back to the land he solemnly promised to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob.” 25 Then Joseph made the sons of Israel swear an oath, and he said, “When God comes to help you and lead you back, you must take my bones with you.” 26 So Joseph died at the age of 110. The Egyptians embalmed him, and his body was placed in a coffin in Egypt.
days.reflection: You live in a time of history where everyone is used to getting everything they want the second they want it. You looked at this idea in the week on the difficulty of delays. But now we see another area of challenge: trusting God for our legacy. A legacy is the impact we leave on others after we are gone. A legacy is really important for a man. It’s kind of a reputation set in stone. We don’t just leave a legacy at the end of our lives; we leave a legacy during each stage of it. You have a legacy as a toddler. Just ask your parents about it. You have a legacy as an elementary-grade kid. Ask your parents about it. And you are developing a legacy as a teenager that will follow you into adulthood. As Joseph comes to the end of his life, he has legacy on his mind. Believe it or not, Joseph has lived to be 110 years old. How crazy is that? In the last 10 weeks, you have seen Joseph start as a teenager with a dream and now he is getting ready to die. God has been faithful to him his entire life, and now he is asking for a specific request from those who come behind him. “When God comes to help you and lead you back, you must take my bones with you.” As much as Joseph has come to see Egypt as his home, he has never forgotten his roots or the promise God made to his family. He wants to make sure he is buried in the land of his fathers. He wants to be remembered with the people of God and not the people of Egypt. He wants to be remembered in God’s story. And guess what! He got his final wish. Though it took hundreds of years (let that sink in), the people were faithful to him. Moses carried his bones out of Egypt. Exodus 13:19 says, “Moses took the bones of Joseph with him, for Joseph had made the sons of Israel swear to do this. He said, 'God will certainly come to help you. When he does, you must take my bones with you from this place.’" And then Joshua buried him in the promised land. Joshua 24:32 says, “The bones of Joseph, which the Israelites had brought along with them when they left Egypt, were buried at Shechem, in the plot of land Jacob had bought from the sons of Hamor for 100 pieces of silver. This land was located in the territory allotted to the descendants of Joseph.” At the end of his life, Joseph left an amazing story behind. A story of pain, risk, faith, joy, leadership, faithfulness, and hope. And the goal of you going through the Primal Path is that you will be able to live out God's plan for your life and leave an amazing legacy of your own. You are well on your way. Keep getting after it.
questions: Ask the person you are doing this study with to share what you were like as a toddler. What was your “toddler legacy?”
What do you want to be remembered for after you leave high school?
What do you want to be remembered for in your life after you die?
What do you think the difference is between a godly legacy and a secular legacy? Why is a godly one important for a man?

thoughts.verse: And I am certain that God, who began the good work within you, will continue his work until it is finally finished on the day when Christ Jesus returns.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to show you some ways you can leave a godly legacy in this season of your life. Ask him for a practical step you can take today.

extras: WEEK 1 0 MANSCHOOL NO FORMATION WITHOUT REPETITION Major congrats here folks! This is the end of the first shift from ease to difficulty. The fact that you have made it this far is really amazing. Most men will go their whole lives without ever studying the life of Joseph. Way to go. SO STRONG. But the goal is not to simply go through the content. It’s to remember it, so God can form you through it. So… This week is a recap week. Spend an evening together going over the big idea of the first shift and talking through the implications of what you have learned through the life of Joseph. 1. EASE (Boys want comfort, life brings difficulty) 2. CONFLICT (People reject us, God never will) 3. CIRCUMSTANCES (Boys complain, men act) 4. TEMPTATION (Boys yield, men resist) 5. DELAY (Boys lose heart, men endure) 6. DISCERNMENT (Boys panic, men pray) 7. RESPONSIBILITY (Boys shrink back, men rise up) 8. RECONCILIATION (Boys stay bitter, men forgive) 9. CHANGE (Boys cling, men adjust) 10. LEGACY (Boys think short term, men long term) Then spend some time reflecting on and discussing your biggest takeaway from this shift. You may want to use some questions like: HEAD What stood out to you most from the life of Joseph? What have you learned about handling difficulty through this study?
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
